title: Eulophus californicus
taxonomy: Eulophus californicus is classified within the order Hymenoptera, which is a group of insects that also includes bees, wasps, and ants. This classification is due to Eulophus californicus being a type of wasp, specifically a parasitic wasp. The family to which Eulophus californicus belongs is Pteromalidae, a family of chalcid wasps. Furthermore, Eulophus californicus is a species within the genus Eulophus, a genus of parasitic wasps. Overall, the classification of Eulophus californicus provides insight into its evolutionary relationships and ecological role.

intro: Eulophus californicus, also known as California penstemon or penstemon, is a perennial flowering plant native to the southwestern United States and northern Mexico, belonging to the Plantaginaceae family. The plant has been traditionally used for its medicinal properties, with reported health benefits including its anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antiseptic properties, which can aid in wound healing and reducing the severity of colds and flu. Eulophus californicus contains bioactive constituents such as flavonoids, phenolic acids, and terpenoids, which contribute to its medicinal properties. The most common herbal preparations made from Eulophus californicus include teas, infusions, and tinctures, which are used to support respiratory health and alleviate symptoms associated with upper respiratory tract infections.
